terms.definition: The measurable degree to which parts of a complex social system act in mutually reinforcing ways toward shared goals.
Adherence to a prescriptive moral norm, distinguished from structural alignment.
Composite operational index aggregating normalized social indicators to approximate systemic coherence.
The perceived right of an institution to command compliance from its members or constituents.
A social configuration in which the married-couple household is treated as the default economic and moral unit, with high switching costs for alternatives.
The ability of a small set of gatekeepers to constrain the entropy of the public information stream.
Maintenance of public alignment through concealment of private deviance rather than behavioral compliance.
Mid-20th-century U.S. collapse of top-decile income share paired with rising median purchasing power.
The 1934–1968 Motion Picture Production Code that imposed moral content rules on U.S. cinema.
The uncertainty or diversity in the cultural information stream; lower entropy implies more uniform messaging.
The combined monitoring and feedback capacity of moral-regulatory institutions within the Theophysics model.
Ordered energy injected into a system to counteract entropy, maintaining structure.
An exogenous infusion of order that delays but does not eliminate systemic decay.
